quick_facts.label: What it is
quick_facts.value: Diploma in Nautical Science (DNS) is a one-year, residential pre-sea programme for the merchant-navy deck-officer pathway; IMU describes its focus as navigation, seamanship and safety management.

quick_facts.label: Academic pathway
quick_facts.value: In the IMU DNS-to-B.Sc. pathway, DNS comprises Semesters I–II. Passing those semesters awards the diploma and permits progression to shipboard training; it is not itself a Deck Officer Certificate of Competency.

quick_facts.label: Core 2026 entry route
quick_facts.value: For the 2026–27 IMU prospectus, the standard school-leaver route is 10+2/equivalent with Physics, Chemistry and Mathematics, at least 60% PCM average, and at least 50% in English in Class 10 or 12.

quick_facts.label: Regulatory setting
quick_facts.value: DNS is among the one-year pre-sea courses within the Directorate General of Shipping (DGS) maritime-training framework; use a DGS-approved institute and verify its current approval before enrolling.

quick_facts.label: Published campus fee context
quick_facts.value: IMU’s published DNS campus fee table lists ₹2,75,000 for the one-year residential programme, but expressly says it does not apply to affiliated institutes. Fees are provider- and intake-specific and should be reconfirmed before payment.

sections.heading: Eligibility
paragraphs: For the 2026–27 intake, IMU lists these main routes: 10+2/equivalent with PCM average of at least 60% and English at least 50% in Class 10 or 12; alternatively, a B.Sc. in Physics, Mathematics, Chemistry or Electronics with Physics in one year and at least 55% in the final year; or a B.E./B.Tech. from IIT or an AICTE-recognised college with at least 50% in the final year. The prospectus also requires at least 50% in English at Class 10, Class 12 or degree level for the graduate-entry routes.

The same 2026–27 prospectus specifies that applicants must be unmarried. It sets the age reference date as August 1, 2026: maximum ages are 25/28/30 for male General/OBC-NCL/SC-ST candidates and 27/30/32 for female General/OBC-NCL/SC-ST candidates. Candidates must also satisfy the prescribed medical/physical-fitness requirements; the DGS framework requires pre-sea medical fitness.
bullets: Do not rely on academic marks alone: obtain the required seafarer medical assessment and confirm the institute’s current admission conditions before accepting an offer.

The DGS’s consolidated training standards also record the baseline DNS pattern of 60% PCM, 50% English, age not over 25 at course commencement, and pre-sea medical fitness; current IMU category-specific rules and prospectus conditions should govern the relevant intake.

sections.heading: Duration and sequence
paragraphs: The pre-sea DNS component is one residential year, delivered as Semesters I and II. In the IMU integrated progression, the broader B.Sc. (Applied Nautical Science) route is described as three years/six semesters: 12 months of pre-sea training, 18 months of structured shipboard training, then a four-month post-sea shore-based Second Mate preparation semester.

Passing the first two semester examinations is required before award of DNS and progression to shipboard training. The later degree and officer-licensing stages have their own sea-time, training, examination and certification requirements.
bullets: Treat “one year” as the duration of the DNS pre-sea diploma, not as the time needed to become a certificated deck officer.
The 18-month onboard phase follows the IMU–DGS Structured Shipboard Training Programme (SSTP).

sections.heading: Curriculum
paragraphs: The pre-sea syllabus combines academic, technical and practical work. Semester I includes applied mathematics and sciences; ship construction and stability; navigation and chartwork; bridge equipment, watchkeeping and meteorology; cargo handling, stowage and seamanship; English and human factors; plus science, computer, workshop and seamanship laboratories.

Semester II advances navigation, bridge equipment/watchkeeping/meteorology, cargo handling and stowage, ship construction and stability, MARPOL and marine-engineering knowledge, emergencies, maritime communications and commercial-shipping knowledge. It also includes communicative-English, workshop/seamanship and navigation practicals.
bullets: The subsequent SSTP/degree syllabus is organised around navigation, cargo handling and stowage, and controlling ship operations/care for persons on board.
Practical competence is central: the programme specifies laboratories, workshop practice and seamanship training rather than classroom study alone.

sections.heading: Admission and institute checks
paragraphs: For the August 2026 affiliated-institute batch, IMU issued a dedicated DNS admissions procedure concerning confirmation of sponsored seats. This signals that applicants should establish whether their offered DNS seat includes shipping-company sponsorship and understand the sponsor’s terms before enrolling.

Apply only to an institute whose relevant DNS course is currently recognised/approved. IMU publishes an affiliated-institute list that identifies DNS providers and sanctioned capacities, while DGS publishes the training guidelines and approvals framework. Approval, affiliation, capacity and admission status can change, so verify them directly with IMU, DGS and the institute for the intended batch.
bullets: Read the current batch prospectus and institute notice for application route, entrance-test requirements, documentation, medical process and deadlines; these can vary by intake.
Keep written evidence of the sponsorship/berth-training arrangement. The post-DNS onboard stage is a required part of the linked progression, not an automatic consequence of holding the diploma.

sections.heading: Costs
paragraphs: As a dated published reference, IMU’s DNS programme page lists ₹2,75,000 as the total annual fee for its one-year residential programme, shown as ₹30,000 programme fee plus ₹1,22,500 in each semester. The page explicitly states that this table is not applicable to affiliated institutes.

Accordingly, ₹2,75,000 should not be treated as a nationwide DNS price. Before committing, request a written, current fee schedule from the specific institute that separates tuition, residential/boarding charges, uniforms and equipment, medical tests, certificates, examination fees and any refundable deposits.
bullets: Compare the total payable amount and refund rules, not merely the advertised tuition fee.
A sponsored seat may have separate commercial and service terms; obtain these in writing from the sponsor and institute.

sections.heading: What comes after DNS
paragraphs: The primary progression is deck-cadet shipboard training under the SSTP. IMU’s guidance specifies 18 months of onboard training after the pre-sea semesters, followed by SSTP assessment and Semesters III–V examinations. After satisfying the applicable requirements, the cadet proceeds to the post-sea Second Mate preparation/examination stage and required STCW courses.

For the academic outcome, IMU states that a candidate who completes the required shipboard/SSTP stages, passes the required IMU semesters and successfully passes the Second Mate examination can qualify for the B.Sc. (Applied Nautical Sciences). The Second Mate (Foreign Going) licence is issued by DGS under the applicable competency framework; it is distinct from the DNS diploma.
bullets: DNS is best understood as entry training for a deck cadet, rather than a stand-alone officer licence.
The SSTP requires documented sea service and assessment; IMU guidance identifies record books, training activities, projects and watchkeeping evidence as part of completion.
